editableNewDialogue.text: In IELTS Speaking Part 3, do you think competition is becoming more intense in schools and workplaces? Answer in 80–110 words, and don’t mention being an AI.

Yes, I think competition has become more intense in both schools and workplaces. In education, students often feel pressure to achieve top grades, build impressive profiles, and outperform others to access limited opportunities. In the workplace, global hiring and rapid technological change mean people must constantly upgrade skills to stay relevant. Social media can also amplify comparison, making success feel more public and urgent. That said, healthy competition can push people to improve, but if it becomes excessive, it may increase stress and reduce cooperation.
Can competition ever be harmful to society? Keep it within 80–110 words.

Competition can be harmful when it encourages unethical behavior or creates a “win at all costs” mindset. For example, companies may cut corners on safety or quality to beat rivals, and individuals might experience burnout from constant pressure to outperform. It can also widen inequality if only those with resources can compete effectively, while others are left behind. However, the problem isn’t competition itself, but poor rules and incentives. With fair regulations and strong social support, competition can remain productive rather than destructive.

By the way, I’m trying to push my score higher, so I’ve been timing myself with a phone stopwatch and forcing my answers to stay within that 80–110-word band. It feels awkward at first, but it’s helping me sound more controlled.

That’s a smart approach. Timing yourself builds pacing, and a strict word range encourages clear structure. You can aim for: a short opening idea, 2–3 supporting points, and a quick concluding sentence. If you keep practicing with the stopwatch, your delivery should become smoother and more natural over time.

Actually, I need to correct something from earlier. I don’t really enjoy playing football anymore. These days I prefer badminton, and I usually play it at a local sports hall rather than an indoor soccer field.

Got it—so it’s not football that you enjoy playing now. You’ve switched to badminton, and you normally play it in a local sports hall, not on an indoor soccer pitch. If you answer the cue card again, focus on badminton and mention that indoor courts suit you better than football did.
